verdict_reasoning: BAX is a canonical pro-apoptotic BCL2 family member. Baseline localization is cytosolic and mitochondrial-outer-membrane (MOM)—where it heterodimerizes with anti-apoptotic BCL2 family members and activates VDAC to promote cytochrome-c release and apoptosis. BAX does not have a membrane-anchor in its baseline state (no TM domain, no GPI). However, during apoptosis and other forms of cell death (necroptosis, pyroptosis), BAX and related pro-apoptotic effectors undergo conformational activation, oligomerize on the MOM, and can be directly exposed on the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane during late apoptosis / secondary necrosis / post-mortem cell permeabilization—when the dying cell's membrane integrity is compromised and formerly internal compartments become accessible from outside. Additionally, BAX has been reported to translocate to the PM during immunogenic cell death (ICD) and viral infection contexts. The protein qualifies as `contextual` via `cell_state_induced` (apoptosis / cell-death-induced display on the outer leaflet), not `no`. While the dominant steady-state compartment is mitochondrial / cytosolic, the documented cell-death-associated surface pool (even though transient and restricted to dying cells) satisfies the gate for `contextual`.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Extent and stability of PM exposure during apoptosis and cell death remains context-dependent; whether this dying-cell transient display is clinically actionable vs. merely permissive exposure during terminal membrane disruption is unresolved.
